segments.s286-g169: از آن پس is a temporal phrase 'from that time on/after that' (از = 'from', آن = 'that', پس = 'after'). خود is an emphatic particle 'myself/I myself' with the personal subject omitted (typical in Persian). از ... باک is a verbless predicate construction meaning 'to have fear of ...' where از marks the object of fear ('of the king of the Turks') and چه باک is a rhetorical/negative exclamation 'what fear' (= 'no fear'). Word order places the object of fear (شاه ترکان) before the noun باک, with چه adding the exclamatory/negating force.
segments.s286-g170: The repeated چه ... و چه ... is a correlative pair meaning 'whether ... or ...' (introducing two alternatives). افراسیاب is a proper name (the Turk king). یک مشت خاک is a noun phrase 'a handful of dust' (یک = 'a/one', مشت = 'handful' as a measure, خاک = 'dust/earth'), used here pejoratively to contrast a great king with something insignificant.
segments.s286-g17: هوا شد is a simple past (or perfective) copular construction: هوا (the air/sky) is the subject and شد (became) is the verb ‘became/turned’. به سان is a fixed comparative phrase meaning ‘like/as’, so the order is Subject + شد + به سان + simile noun (پرند بنفش). بنفش postmodifies پرند as an adjective (purple bird).
segments.s286-g18: ز here is the poetic preposition ‘from/because of’ introducing the cause; تابیدن is a masdar/infinitive used in a causative sense ‘the shining’ (i.e., the shining of the banner). کاویانی specifies the banner’s dynastic/legendary affiliation and درفش is the noun ‘banner/standard’ that completes the causal phrase.
segments.s286-g19: درفشی here functions as a noun meaning ‘a standard/banner’ (poetic form). به بالای is a prepositional phrase ‘to/on top of’; فارسی word order places the locative phrase after the noun: [standard] + به بالای + [سرو سهی] (the tall/dark cypress). سهی is a poetic epithet modifying سرو.
segments.s286-g20: پدید آمد is a light-verb compound meaning ‘appeared’ (پدید + آمد). از دور is an adverbial phrase ‘from afar’ placed after the verb for poetic effect. با introduces an accompanying manner (‘with’) and فرهی is an attributive noun meaning ‘nobility/lofty bearing’ describing how it came.
segments.s286-g21: به گردش is an adverbial nominal phrase meaning ‘at the turning/circuit/motion of…’; it licenses the genitive noun phrase that follows. سواران is the plural noun ‘riders/horsemen’ and جوشنوران is a descriptive plural attached to them (poetic term referring to their mail/armour or martial brilliance), forming a genitive/attributive chain.
segments.s286-g22: زمین شد is again Subject + شد ‘became/turned’. بنفش is the predicative adjective ‘purple’. از کران تا کران is a fixed idiom ‘from edge to edge’ (lit. ‘from shore/limit to shore/limit’), where از marks the start point and تا marks the extent.
segments.s286-g23: پس is a discourse marker ‘then/so’. هر +درفشی درفشی is a poetic repetition (هر درفشی درفشی) meaning ‘each standard’ (the doubled form emphasizes individuality or multiplicity). به پای is a locative phrase ‘at the foot/base of’, where پای is used figuratively for ‘foot/base of (the standard)’.
segments.s286-g24: The correlative/contrastive construction چه ... و چه ... here means ‘whether ... or ...’. از marks source/material (‘of/from the dragon’) and پیکر همای is the alternative image ‘the figure/form of the Huma (mythical bird)’, completing the contrast.
segments.s286-g9: که is the subordinating particle ‘that/when’ linking this clause to what precedes. اندر زمان is an archaic locative phrase meaning ‘at that time’ or ‘in the moment’ (اندر = in/within, زمان = moment/ time). از لب دیده‌بان is a prepositional phrase ‘from the lip of the watchman’ where دیده‌بان (sentinel) is in apposition to لب without an overt ezāfe because of poetic compression.
segments.s286-g10: به گوش آمد is an impersonal/experiential construction ‘came to the ear’ (آمد = came/arrived). از کوه marks source ‘from the mountain’. زیبد here qualifies فغان and functions like an adjective/predicate (‘striking/beautiful’); the adjective follows the noun in this poetic order (a common classical pattern). فغان = lament/cry.
segments.s286-g11: که again introduces the clause. از گرد شد دشت is a compressed way to say ‘the plain became (covered) with dust’: گرد = dust (here noun) governed by از and شد = became. چون تیره شب is a simile placed after the clause: ‘like a dark night’ (چون = like; تیره شب = dark night).
segments.s286-g12: شگفتی (a marvel/wonder) is the subject and برآمد is the verb ‘arose/issued forth’. ز هر سو = ‘from every side/direction’. جلب is a noun related to attraction/bringing — here functioning as a nominal sense ‘wondrous sights/attractions’ that issue forth from all sides (poetic, compressed noun phrase).
segments.s286-g13: خروشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive ‘to roar’ used nominally; followed by the instrument (کوس = war-drum) so the phrase reads ‘the roaring of the drum’. با کرنای = ‘with (the) horns/trumpets’ where با marks the accompanying instrument.
segments.s286-g14: بجنباند is a transitive present-stem verb meaning ‘sets in motion/causes to move’ (here in third-person form). آن دشت is the direct object ‘that plain’. گویی is a comparative/particle ‘as if’ or ‘it seemed’, and ز جای ‘from its place’ completes the simile (‘as if it were moved from its place’). Note poetic inversion: verb before object and the simile tacked on at the end.
segments.s286-g15: یکی is an indefinite numeral ‘one/a certain’. تخت پیروزه is a noun + modifier phrase ‘turquoise throne’ (poetically the ezāfe is omitted). بر پشت پیل = ‘on the back of the elephant’ with بر = on and پشت پیل = elephant’s back (noun + noun construction without an explicit ezāfe for brevity).
segments.s286-g16: درفشان here functions adjectivally as ‘shining/glittering’ (rather than the literal ‘standard-bearer’) describing the throne or its display. به کردار = ‘in the manner/appearance of’ (به = in, کردار = manner/appearance). دریای نیل = ‘the sea of nīl’ where نیل is used as the deep indigo/blue color — ‘like the indigo sea’ — a poetic simile for brilliance/color.
segments.s286-g121: ز پیمان نگردند ایرانیان: Word order is poetic: the prepositional phrase ز پیمان ('from the covenant') comes before the verbal negative نگردند ('do not turn') and the subject ایرانیان ('the Iranians') is placed at the end. نگردند is present subjunctive/indicative negative of گردیدن/گشتن meaning 'do not turn/break away' here; together the clause says the Iranians do not abandon the covenant.
segments.s286-g122: از این در کنون نیست بیم زیان: از این ('because of/from this', referring to the foregoing clause) governs the whole assertion. در کنون نیست is a negated existential statement 'there is not now/at present', with کنون ('now') placed between preposition and copula for emphasis. بیم زیان is a noun phrase 'fear (of) loss' (بیم governed by the negation نیست).
segments.s286-g123: سه کار است پیش‌آمده ناگزیر: 'سه کار است' is a basic existential 'there are three affairs'. پیش‌آمده is a past participle/adjective 'have arisen/occurred' modifying کار, and ناگزیر ('inevitable') further qualifies them; the copula است links them—'three unavoidable things have arisen.'
segments.s286-g124: همه گوش دارید برنا و پیر: همه ('all, everyone') is followed by گوش دارید literally 'you have ears'—an idiom meaning 'listen' or 'pay attention' with دارید as second-person plural present. برنا و پیر ('young and old') is an inclusive apposition describing the audience addressed by دارید.
segments.s286-g125: اگرتان به زنهار باید شدن: اگرتان is the conditional 'if (you)' (اگر with the second-person plural/enclitic تان). به زنهار is a dative phrase 'to/for warning' meaning 'to take warning' or 'to heed the warning'. باید شدن uses شدن as an infinitival/verbal complement with باید 'it is necessary', yielding an impersonal/modal sense: 'if it is necessary for you to take warning.'
segments.s286-g126: کنونتان همی رای باید زدن: کنونتان ('now for you'—کنون with the possessive/enclitic) sets the time; همی is an emphatic particle ('indeed/just' here). رای باید زدن places the noun رای ('counsel/plan') before the verb زدن in classical order: with باید 'must' marking necessity, and زدن used idiomatically with رای meaning 'to express/offer a counsel'—'you must now put forth counsel.'
segments.s286-g127: وگر بازگشتن به خرگاه خویش: وگر is 'if / but if'; بازگشتن is the infinitive 'to return'. The prepositional phrase به خرگاه خویش ('to their own donkey-stall/home') follows—خویش is expressed as خوی + ش (possessive clitic). The line presents an alternative: 'or, returning to their stables...' (خرگاه used in a demeaning sense).
segments.s286-g128: سپردن به نیک و به بد راه خویش: سپردن is the infinitive 'to entrust/hand over'. به نیک و به بد ('to the good and to the bad') indicates the recipients/forces (i.e. fortune or misfortune). راه خویش ('one's own way/path') is the object possessed by the clitic; the whole phrase means 'to entrust one's path to good or to ill.'
segments.s286-g1: چو is the subordinating temporal conjunction 'when'. The clause uses verbless predication with نزدیک ... شدند: نزدیک (adverb/adjective) + شدند (past plural of شدن, 'became/approached'), so literally 'when (they) became near'. The coordinated nouns بنگاه و لشکر are the things becoming near (word order: locative/goal before the verb).
segments.s286-g2: This is a short main clause in which پذیره functions as 'reception/welcome' (a noun used with the agentive title), followed by the agent سپهبد and the patient سپاه; آمدند is the past plural 'they came'. Together the phrase conveys 'the spahbed received/welcomed the army (as they came)'. Word order (noun phrases before آمدند) is typical of older Persian.
segments.s286-g3: به پیش سپه is a prepositional phrase 'before/in front of the host' (به + پیش). بود is the past copula 'was', so the clause literally reads 'before the host was Gostaham the lion' — a topical inversion placing the locative phrase first. گستهم appears here as the proper name (appositive to شیر).
segments.s286-g4: بیامد is the past form 'he came' or 'he advanced'; بر is the preposition marking direction/target 'upon/to'; پهلوان دلیر is the noun plus epithet 'the brave champion'. The verb precedes the goal phrase, a common older Persian order: 'he came upon the brave champion.'
segments.s286-g5: زمین + را: را is the definite/direct-object marker marking زمین as the object. ببوسید is the simple past 'he kissed'. و کرد آفرین is a light-verb construction: کرد ('made/did/uttered') + آفرین (exclamation) = 'he exclaimed “Bravo!”'.
segments.s286-g6: سپاهت is the noun سپاه with the 2nd-person possessive suffix '-ت' 'your army'; بی‌آزار is an adjective after the noun 'harmless'. گفتا is the archaic past 'said' (often used to introduce direct speech), followed by the imperative/command-like ببین 'see/behold'. The sequence renders direct speech: 'He said, "Behold — your harmless army."'
segments.s286-g7: چنانچون is the comparative/temporal 'just as' introducing a clause of manner. سپردی is second-person past 'you entrusted' and سپردم is first-person past 'I entrusted' — the pair contrasts the actions of speaker and addressee. به هم here means 'to/among them' or 'likewise' (an adverbial/recipient shifter). The clause thus means 'As you entrusted, I entrusted as well.'
segments.s286-g8: در این is a locative/idiomatic 'in this (matter, respect)'. بود is the past copula 'was'. گودرز با گستهم is a simple accompaniment clause 'Goudarz was with Gostaham' (با marks accompaniment). Word order places the locative first, then the copula, then the accompaniment phrase.
segments.s286-g25: 'اگر' introduces a conditional clause ('if'); 'همچنین' modifies the manner ('so / like that'); 'تیزرانی' is a deverbal/nominal form meaning swift movement or haste used adverbially ('hasten, swift riding'); and 'کنند' is the third-person plural present/subjunctive form of کردن, here marking the potential action ('they do/hasten'). Together they form the protasis 'if they hurry in that way.'
segments.s286-g26: 'به یک روز دیگر' is a temporal phrase meaning 'in/after one more day' placed before the directional adverb 'بدینجا' ('to this place'); 'رسند' is the third-person plural present/subjunctive/potential of رسیدن ('they reach/arrive') and is the apodosis linked to the conditional 'اگر ... کنند'. The clause as a whole says they will reach this place after one day if they hurry.
segments.s286-g27: The preposition 'ز' (from) with 'کوه' ('mountain') gives the source/place. 'کنابد' is an archaic/present-form verb meaning 'suffers' or 'is in distress/toils' describing the watchman; word order is inverted for poetic effect so the subject 'همان دیده‌بان' ('that same watchman') follows the verb and is identified by 'همان'.
segments.s286-g28: 'بدید' is the simple past third-person singular 'he saw' taking 'آن شگفتی' ('that marvel/wonder') as its object. The conjunction 'و' links this clause to the following action 'آمد دوان' where 'آمد' is past 'he came' and 'دوان' is a manner adverb/participial form meaning 'running/with haste' — 'came running.'
segments.s286-g29: 'چنین گفت‌گر' is a concessive/conditional phrase meaning 'if one says thus' or 'were someone to say that.' The following nominal phrase 'چشم من تیره نیست' is a present negated predication: 'my eye is not dark/obscured' (چشم = eye/vision; تیره = dark/obscured; نیست negates the predicate).
segments.s286-g30: Here 'وز' should be read as the fused 'و ز' (= 'and from'), linking the next predicate; 'اندوه دیدار من' is a genitive phrase 'the sorrow of my meeting' (i.e. sorrow at the meeting), and 'خیره نیست' is again a negated predicate: 'is not dazed/stunned.' So the clause states 'and I am not dazed by the sorrow of our meeting.'
segments.s286-g31: Word order is inverted for emphasis: 'ز ترکان' ('upon/against the Turks' — poetic use of ز) marks the affected party; 'ایزد' (God) is the agent, and 'برآورد دمار' is a past action meaning 'brought/caused ruin' (برآورد here is the classical past of برآوردن 'to cause, bring upon'). The clause means 'God brought ruin on the Turks.'
segments.s286-g32: 'همه رنجشان' ('all their suffering') is the subject; the idiom 'سر به سر' means 'one by one / all together' and modifies how the change occurs; 'گشت' is past 'became' and 'خوار' is the predicate adjective 'despised/humbled.' Together: 'all their hardships, one and all, became despised / they were utterly humbled.'
segments.s286-g153: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. ترکان is the noun 'the Turks' (subject). شنیدند is past tense 3rd-person-plural 'they heard'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. ایشان is the pronoun 'them' (the people previously mentioned). سخن is the noun 'speech' or 'words' and is the object of شنیدند.
segments.s286-g154: یکی نیک پاسخ is a noun phrase 'one good answer/reply'. فگندند is an archaic past 3rd-person-plural form (classical spelling of فکندند) meaning 'they threw/uttered' — here used figuratively for 'they gave/let loose'. بن at the end is a short poetic particle meaning 'thereupon/then' (not the literal noun 'root'), so it functions adverbially; because the provided noun senses don't match this usage, its sense is marked -1.
segments.s286-g155: که introduces a result/relative clause 'so that/that'. سالار با ده یل نامدار is a noun phrase 'the commander with ten renowned heroes' (سالار = commander; با = 'with'; ده یل = 'ten heroes'; نامدار = 'renowned') and functions as the noun phrase linked to the verb in the next segment (either subject or direct object, depending on verb voice).
segments.s286-g156: کشیدند is past 3rd-person-plural 'they dragged/drew' (کشیدن = to pull/drag). کشته is the noun 'the slain' (object). بر is the preposition 'upon/onto'. آن گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in that manner/that way' (گونه = 'manner'). خوار is an adjective 'despised/ignoble' describing the state in which they were left. Together: 'they dragged the slain upon/into that ignoble manner.'
segments.s286-g157: وز آن روی is an idiomatic, somewhat archaic phrase meaning 'for that reason/therefore' (وز here is an archaic/formal connective related to از; آن روی = 'that face/side/reason'). کیخسرو is the proper name 'Kay Khosrow'. آید پدید is a present/habitual-classical sequence 'comes/appears' (آید = 'comes'; پدید = 'to view/appear'), meaning 'came forth / appeared.'
segments.s286-g158: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'. یارد here means 'the companion/friend' (یار + dative/poetic form). بدین is the prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in this' or 'at this' (written here as ب + دین in the segmentation; it means 'in/at this'). رزمگاه is 'battlefield'. آرمید is past 'rested' and in epic diction often euph. for 'fell/died' (lit. 'laid to rest'). The clause thus says that 'a companion rested/died at this battlefield.'
segments.s286-g159: The pattern نه ... و ... و نه repeats negation 'neither ... nor ...'. اسب = 'horse'. سلیح is an archaic/spelling-variant form of سلاح = 'weapon'. پای = 'foot' (here likely meaning 'troops' or 'stance/limb'). پر = 'feather/plume' (poetic emblem for wing/plume). The segment lists items that were absent.
segments.s286-g160: Continuation of the negated list: نه گنج 'no treasure', و نه سالار 'nor commander', و نه نامور 'nor famed person/renown'. The repeated نه negates each noun in turn; نامور is an adjective/noun 'renowned/famous'.
segments.s286-g73: که‌گر is an archaic conditional 'که گر' meaning 'if'. من شوم کشته uses شوم (subjunctive/present of شدن) plus the past participle کشته to form a passive idea: 'I should become killed' = 'if I am slain'. بر کینه‌گاه is a prepositional phrase ('on/at the seat/occasion of hatred') giving the place or motive of the killing.
segments.s286-g74: شما کس مباشید is a negative imperative addressed to a plural/formal 'you': مباشید = 'do not be'. The construction means 'let none of you be...' (i.e., 'do not any of you become/stand'). پیش سپاه is a locative 'before/in front of the army'.
segments.s286-g75: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. کشته گردم pairs the participle کشته with گردم (from گشتن used as a passive/become) to mean 'if I am slain'. بر این دشت indicates the location 'on this plain'. کین stands alone as the subject/theme 'vengeance/hatred' that the next clause will predicate upon.
segments.s286-g76: شود is the impersonal/3rd‑person form 'it will become / will be'. تنگ here is predicative, 'tight/pressing/severe' — i.e. 'vengeance will press hard'. بر names the target of that pressure ('upon/against'), and نامداران زمین 'the renowned men of the land' is the group affected.
segments.s286-g77: نه negates the whole clause: 'not / there will not'. از تخمه ویسه is a genitive phrase 'from the seed/offspring of Viseh'. ماند (past/perfect) 'remains' and کسی ('anyone' but here negated = 'no one') completes the idea: 'no one of Viseh's seed will remain.'
segments.s286-g78: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. اندر سرش = 'in his head' (اندر = in, سر = head, -ش = his). مغز باشد بسی combines مغز 'brain/intelligence' with باشد (present/subjunctive of بودن used as 'has/is') and بسی 'much/enough' — 'who has enough/ample brain in his head.'
segments.s286-g79: که here begins an explanatory clause ('for / that'), and بر کینه‌گه gives the place 'at/on the spot of hatred'. چونک is a temporal/causal conjunction ('when / since'), ما را is the object marker attached to 'us', and کشند (3rd plural subjunctive/present) is the verb 'they kill' used here for a hypothetical/future event.
segments.s286-g80: چو is another temporal conjunction 'when'. سرهای ما is the object 'our heads' (سرهای = heads, ما = our). سوی ایران 'toward Iran' gives direction, and کشند (3rd pl.) is the verb 'they carry / they will take' — typical SOV order places object + direction before the verb in poetry.
segments.s286-g161: The correlative negation 'نه ... و نه ...' (= 'neither ... nor ...') denies two nouns. 'نیروی جنگ' is a genitive compound meaning 'the force/strength of war' (i.e. military strength). 'راه گریز' is a compound noun 'way of escape' (راه = path/way, گریز = escape). The segment lists what is lacking.
segments.s286-g162: 'چه' here is an exclamatory/interrogative 'what/how' introducing the question 'what can be done?'. 'با خویشتن' = 'with oneself'. 'کرد' is the verb 'to do' used in a verbless/exclamatory construction ('what to do'); word order is inverted for poetic effect. 'باید' is the modal 'must/it is necessary', and 'ستیز' (used verbally here) means 'to fight/struggle' — so the clause expresses that, given no strength or escape, one must fight (literally: 'what with oneself to do — one must fight').
segments.s286-g163: 'اگر' introduces the conditional 'if'. 'بازگردیم' is the verb 'to return/come back' (here used for 'they return' in the classical/poetic word order). 'گودرز و شاه' are the coordinated subjects 'Goudarz and the king' placed after the verb for poetic inversion.
segments.s286-g164: 'پس' = 'then/therefore'. 'ما' is the object 'us' (accusative), placed before the verb for emphasis. 'برانند' is a third‑person plural present/subjunctive verb 'they will drive/drive away'. 'پیل و سپاه' is the subject ('elephant and army') placed after the verb (topicalized) — so: 'then the elephant and the army will drive us away.'
segments.s286-g165: 'رهایی نیابیم' is a negative subjunctive/formal present meaning 'we do not (may not) obtain/find release/freedom' — 'نیابیم' here is the subjunctive/optative form. 'یک تن' = 'one person' (i.e. 'not even one person'), and 'به جان' literally 'to life', qualifying that none will be left alive. Together: 'we shall not find freedom/release for even one person alive.'
segments.s286-g166: Again using correlative negation 'نه ... و نه ...' = 'neither ... nor ...'. 'خرگاه' means a lowly shelter/place (here 'refuge/dwelling'), and 'بینیم' is the verb 'we see/shall see' (present/subjunctive form). 'دودمان' means 'lineage/dynasty/family' — so 'we shall see neither shelter nor lineage.'
segments.s286-g167: 'به زنهار' is an interjection meaning 'take heed' or 'be warned' (a conventional admonition). 'بر ما' = 'upon us/for us', 'کنون' = 'now', and 'عار نیست' = 'there is no shame/disgrace' (literally 'shame is not'). The phrase means something like 'heed this: for us there is no disgrace now.'
segments.s286-g168: 'سپاه است بسیار' = 'there is a great/many army' (سپاه subject + است copula + بسیار 'many'). The second clause 'و سالار نیست' adds 'and there is no commander' (سالار = leader/commander; نیست negates existence). The two clauses contrast plenty of troops with lack of leadership.
segments.s286-g57: همی is a literary iterative/adverbial particle here meaning “kept / repeatedly”; زار modifies the verb as an adverb ‘lamentingly / in sorrow’; گفتند is the past plural ‘they said/ cried’; کای is the poetic vocative particle ‘O’ that introduces the address; نره شیر is a vocative compound ‘male lion’ (a metaphorical address to a brave man). Word order puts the iterative/adverbial before the verb and the vocative after it.
segments.s286-g58: This string is a sequence of titles/appositions describing the addressee: سپهدار (‘commander’) + پیران (here used as a proper name/title) + سوار (‘rider, horseman’) + دلیر (‘brave’). In classical Persian such noun clusters stack as epithets; پیران functions as a name (not the common noun sense).
segments.s286-g59: چه is the interrogative ‘what’; بایست (from بایستن) here expresses what ought/should be done or what is fitting; آن points to the person/quality just spoken of; رادی and راستی are paired qualities — ‘generosity/magnanimity’ and ‘uprightness/truthfulness’ — used as abstract nouns describing that person.
segments.s286-g60: چو introduces a subordinate temporal/causal clause ‘when / since’; رفتن is the infinitive ‘to go’ used idiomatically for ‘to depart (life)’; ز گیتی = ‘from the world / from life’ (poetic preposition ز); چنین is ‘thus / in this way’; خواستی is the simple past second-person ‘you wished / desired’. The finite verb خواستی comes at the end, as typical in Persian subordinate clauses.
segments.s286-g61: کنون = ‘now’; کام دشمن is a possessive/genitive phrase ‘the enemy’s desire/wish’; برآمد (past) means ‘arose / came to pass / was realized’; همه at the end is emphatic ‘altogether / completely’. The clause says the enemy’s wish has now been realized.
segments.s286-g62: به بد بر تو is a prepositional phrase ‘to/for you, in misfortune / for the worse’; گیتی سرآمد is a nominal phrase meaning ‘the world's end / the end of the world’ (سرآمد = conclusion/end) used predicatively; همه again adds emphasis ‘all / entirely’. Together the clause says that, alas, the world has come to its end for you or everything has turned against you.
segments.s286-g63: که here is the interrogative relative ‘who’; جوید is the literary present ‘seeks / will seek’; کنون = ‘now’; در جهان = ‘in the world’; کین تو = ‘your kīn’ — kīn denotes vengeance/hatred here (object of جوید). The clause is a rhetorical question about who will pursue or desire vengeance for/against you now.
segments.s286-g64: که = ‘who’; گیرد (from گرفتن) is the present literary form ‘takes / seizes / adopts’; کنون ‘now’; راه و آیین تو = ‘your way and your custom/practice’ (آیین here = customs/practice). The clause asks rhetorically who will now adopt or uphold the departed person’s path and customs.
segments.s286-g81: This phrase places the agent/source first with the poetic preposition ز (here 'by'/'from'), then the name گودرز, followed by خواهد (3rd-person present/future auxiliary 'will'/'does'), the noun سپه 'host/army', and the admonition زینهار ('beware'/'take heed'). Grammatically the construction reads as a proclamation: 'By Goudarz the host gives a warning' (i.e. Goudarz warns the army).
segments.s286-g82: Imperative/admonitive content addressed to the audience: شما 'you' as addressee, خویشتن را the emphatic object 'yourselves' marked by را, مدارید a present-tense imperative/negativeable verb meaning 'do (not) make/consider/spare' (here 'do not make'), and خوار the predicative adjective 'despised/disgraced.' Word order is emphatic: 'You — your selves — do not make (yourselves) despised.'
segments.s286-g83: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject; راه سوی بیابان برید is a poetic verbal phrase where راه + بریدن = 'to take the road' or 'set out.' سوی بیابان gives the direction 'toward the desert/wilderness.' برید is past tense here ('they set out').
segments.s286-g84: مگر introduces an exception ('except'); کز is the contracted relative 'که + از' meaning 'who/whom' or 'that from whom'; بد دشمنان 'evil/ill enemies' modifies جان (life), and برید (again past) is used metaphorically/'literally' as 'cut off/took' — i.e. 'whom the wicked enemies had taken the life of' = 'whom the wicked enemies had slain.'
segments.s286-g85: به لشکرگه خویش 'to their camp' uses به + place (لشکرگه) and the possessive خویش (here split into خوی + ش) 'their/own.' رفتند is the past plural 'they went/they returned,' and باز after the verb gives the sense 'back/again' — 'they went back to their own camp.'
segments.s286-g86: A compact nominal clause describing condition: همه 'all/everyone' as topic, دیده 'eyes' and دل 'hearts' as the two members, each modified by پر + noun meaning 'full (of) X' (پر خون = 'full of blood'; پر گداز = 'full of anguish/ardor'). The sequence lists symptoms: 'eyes full of blood and hearts full of anguish.'
segments.s286-g87: بدانست (past tense 'knew/perceived') precedes the subject in this poetic order; لشکر سراسر همه = 'the army, all throughout' or 'the whole army' — سراسر modifies لشکر to mean 'entire/throughout.' The clause reads 'the whole army knew/realized.'
segments.s286-g88: A subordinate clause introduced by که 'that': شد 'became' + بی‌شبان 'without shepherds' describes the state; آن گرازان رمه 'that herd of boars' is the patient/subject of the state. Literally: 'that that herd of boars became shepherdless' = 'that the herd of boars had been left without shepherds.'
segments.s286-g89: همه سر به سر is an idiomatic reduplication meaning “one and all / everyone together.” زار و گریان are two coordinated predicative adjectives/participles: زار (lamenting/wretched) and گریان (weeping). شدند is the past plural of شدن “to become,” so the whole clause means “they became [such].
segments.s286-g90: چو is the temporal conjunction “when/as.” بر + آتش = “upon the fire.” تیز qualifies آتش as “sharp/keen/fierce.” بریان is a past participle/adjective meaning “roasted/burnt.” شدند again is the past plural of شدن, yielding a passive/sta‑te meaning “were/ became roasted.”
segments.s286-g91: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase meaning “to/near.” Here it governs the following proper names; the phrase places the action near the persons Lahaak and Farshidvard. فرشیدورد is a personal name; لهاک in this line is most likely a proper name as well (treated here as a place/person), not the abstract sense listed elsewhere.
segments.s286-g92: برفتند is the past plural of رفتن “to go” (they went/proceeded). با introduces the instrument/ accompaniment “with.” دل پر از means “a heart full of …”; پر is the adjective “full (of).” از marks the content (from/of), باد here is the noun “wind,” and سرد qualifies it as “cold.” The whole clause: “they went … with hearts filled with cold wind.”
segments.s286-g93: که introduces an interrogative/relative clause here and functions like “that/for / …” but in context leads into the question. اکنون = “now.” چه سازیم is present-subjunctive/optative in form (سازیم, from ساختن/ساخت) used as “what shall we do” or “what can we do.” ز این رزمگاه = “of/from this battlefield” (ز = poetic from/of).
segments.s286-g94: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” شد is past of شدن “became.” پهلوان = “champion/hero.” پشت here means “rear/behind/support” (the rear of an army). توران سپاه = “the Turanian host/army” (genitive relation: the host of Turan). Grammatically this is a temporal subordinate clause: “when a champion became the rear of Turan’s army.”
segments.s286-g95: چنین = “thus/so.” گفت is the past tense “said” (here used as a verb). هر کس که = “whoever / every one who.” پیران گرد: پیران = “the elders”; گرد is an adverbial/relational element meaning “round/about/among” (i.e. “who was among the elders” or “who had gathered with the elders”). The clause = “Thus said anyone who stood among the elders.”
segments.s286-g96: جز = “except.” از marks origin/partitive “from/of.” نام نیکو = “good name” = reputation/fame. ز گیهان = “from the world” (ز = poetic from; گیهان = the world). نبرد in this line functions as a verbal idea “he did not take/carry away” (classical usage); the lexical list given treats نبرد as noun “battle,” so here its sense in the wordlist does not fit and is marked accordingly.
segments.s286-g49: "برفتند" is the simple past, 3rd-person plural of رفتن (they went/they departed). The two following nouns, "لهاک" and "فرشیدورد", are coordinated subjects in post-verbal position (typical of epic word order): "X and Y went."
segments.s286-g50: "بدانجا" = 'to that place' (preposition+demonstrative), and "که" introduces a relative clause 'where/which'. "بد جایگاه نبرد" is a noun phrase 'the place of battle / the battlefield' (بد here is an attributive/poetic element marking the site as bad/ill-fated or simply forming part of the phrase). The whole segment reads 'to that place which was the site of the battle.'
segments.s286-g51: "بدیدند" is the past tense, 3rd-person plural of دیدن (they saw). The phrase "کشته به دیدار خویش" functions as the object: "a slain one (کشته) in their sight / before them." Here "به + دیدار" conveys location/position in sight and "خویش + ش" is the possessive clitic ('their') attached in two pieces (خوی + ش).
segments.s286-g52: This is an appositive noun phrase identifying the slain: "سپهبد" (spahbed) followed by the descriptor "برادر جهاندار خویش" — 'the brother of their sovereign.' Word order is head (سپهبد) then appositive/genitive phrase (برادر ... خویش), with the possessive clitic again as ش attached to خوی.
segments.s286-g53: This is a nominal phrase describing the number and type of riders. "ابا ده سوار" corresponds to (archaic/spell variant of) "با ده سوار" = 'with ten horsemen' (preposition + numeral + noun). "آن گزیده سران" follows as a descriptive apposition: "those chosen chiefs." The demonstrative "آن" points to the specific group already mentioned.
segments.s286-g54: "ز" is the poetic/genitive preposition 'from/of'. The phrase "ز ترکان دلیران جنگاوران" lists origin and qualities: 'from the Turks' (ترکان) followed by two coordinating adjectives/nouns 'دلیران' (the brave) and 'جنگاوران' (warriors/fighters). The sequence is a string of nouns/adjectives in apposition describing the ten horsemen.
segments.s286-g55: "بر آن دیده" = 'at that sight' (preposition "بر" + demonstrative "آن" + noun "دیده" meaning sight/eye). The following "بر زار و جوشان شدند" uses شدن in past plural (شدند) meaning 'they became' and predicates two state adjectives: "زار" (lamenting/wretched) and "جوشان" (seething/boiling with anger). The repeated preposition "بر" here marks 'at/with respect to' the emotional reaction to the sight.
segments.s286-g56: "ز خون برادر خروشان شدند" is causal/result clause: "ز" = 'from/because of', "خون برادر" = 'the brother's blood' (genitive), and "خروشان شدند" uses the adjective "خروشان" ('roaring/tumultuous') with شدند 'they became' — 'they were in tumult because of their brother's blood.'
segments.s286-g113: بدان گیتی is a preposed locative phrase ('in that world'); ب here is the prefix be- attached to دان to make the demonstrative/locative sense 'that' (archaic/poetic). افتاد is a simple past 3rd-sg verb meaning 'befell/happened to'; نیک و بدش is a coordinated noun phrase 'his good and (his) bad', with the enclitic ش marking possession attached to بد (understood to govern both nouns). Word order is locative + verb + object (typical older Persian Poetic order).
segments.s286-g114: همانا که begins an emphatic clause ('indeed that...'). نیک است is a nominal predicate ('is good'); با introduces accompaniment/agency and takes ایزدش ('his God' / his deity) as the complement. ایزدش is possessed by the suffix -ش. The clause states a general truth: 'the good is with his God.'
segments.s286-g115: بس از لشکر خویش is an adverbial phrase meaning 'much/regarding his own army' (بس = ample/much; از + لشکر خویش = 'concerning/from his army'); تیمار خورد is a light-verb construction where تیمار 'care' combines with خورد (here functioning as a light past verb) to mean 'took care of / was solicitous for.' The possessive suffix -ش attaches to خوی (خوی + ش = خویش 'his own').
segments.s286-g116: ز introduces the agent/source 'from Goudarz'; پیمان ستد is a verbal phrase in past tense where ستد means 'exacted/took' (here 'exacted/obtained' a pledge). در نبرد is a locative adverbial 'in the battle.' The whole means he exacted a pledge from Goudarz during the fight.
segments.s286-g117: که‌گر is the conditional 'if'; شوم is 1st-sg present/subjunctive 'I become / I should be' used here in a conditional to mean 'if I am'; کشته is a past participle 'slain' functioning as the predicate; در کینه‌گاه is a locative phrase 'in the place/seat of hatred' (i.e., where enmity or ambush occurs).
segments.s286-g118: نجویی تو is a negative exhortation addressed to 'you': نجویی = ن + جویی (do not seek/pursue) with the final -ی marking the 2nd‑sg form; کین is the object 'vengeance'. زان سپس 'from/after that' links this warning to what follows; با سپاه is an adverbial 'with an army.' Overall: 'do not you seek vengeance, nor afterward (do so) with an army.'
segments.s286-g119: گذرشان دهی is an exhortation 'you should give them passage / let them pass' (گذر as 'passage' with the verbal دهی 'you give'). تا introduces purpose/result 'so that/in order that'; به توران شوند uses شوند (subjunctive 3rd‑pl of شدن) to indicate 'they may reach/go to Turan.'
segments.s286-g120: کمین را is the definite object 'the ambush' (را marks it); نسازی is a negative present/subjunctive 'do not make/set' (ن + سازی from سازیدن/ساختن); بریشان = بر ایشان 'against them' (classical contraction) and کمند 'snare' forms the second object. The clause forbids laying an ambush or setting a snare against them.
segments.s286-g137: ز (a poetic preposition) + هرگونه marks 'from/of every kind'; رانیم is 1st person plural present stem (راند‑ + ‑یم) meaning 'we drive/drive off'; یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/utterly' and سخن here is a discourse adverbial 'in a word / in short'. Word order is topical/prepositional (ز هرگونه) followed by the verbal predicate and then the adverbial gloss (یکسر سخن).
segments.s286-g138: جز از خواست یزدان نباشد ز بن is a compact clause: جز از + خواست یزدان = 'except from the will of God'; نباشد is the negative of the copula/verb 'باشد' used here to mean 'there is not / there is nothing'; ز بن = 'from the root / from the foundation'. The whole clause states the general principle that nothing originates except by God's will. Note the classical negative copula نباشد (subjunctive/optative form) used idiomatically.
segments.s286-g139: ور is the conditional particle 'if'; ایدون here (an older/formal form) carries a sense of 'now/at present/it appears' (a temporal/grounding word); کتان appears as a noun before رای and functions as the topic/subject controlling 'رای شهرست' (the phrase means 'the city's decision/opinion'); شهرست is 'شهر' + the copular suffix '-ست' ('is the city' as an attributive to رای). The final و گاه adds 'and the time/occasion' — so syntactically this segment sets a conditional circumstance ('if now the town's counsel and the occasion are such').
segments.s286-g140: همانا is an emphatic 'verily/indeed'; که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/which'; بر ما is the prepositional phrase 'against/us'; نگیرند is 3rd person plural present-subjunctive/negative of گرفتن meaning 'they do not seize/they do not block' (here 'they will not bar/stop'); راه is the object 'the way/path'. The clause thus asserts confidently that under those circumstances 'they will not obstruct our way.'
segments.s286-g141: وگرتان is the conditional particle وگر(تـان) 'and/or but if'; به زنهار شاه is a prepositional phrase 'at/by the king's admonition/warning' (زنهار is a noun 'warning, admonition'); است is the copula 'is' and رای is the noun 'counsel/decision'. Together the segment means 'and if, by the king's warning, the decision is [so]'.
segments.s286-g142: بباید is an impersonal/modal verb 'one must / it is necessary'; بسیچید is a verbal form meaning 'to muster/assemble' (here an infinitival or substantive verbal form used as the complement of بباید — 'must assemble'); و + رفتن ز جای links a coordinated action: رفتن is the infinitive 'to go/leave' and ز جای 'from the place'. The structure is 'one must gather (troops) and (be) going/leave the place.'
segments.s286-g143: وگرتان is the conditional 'and/but if'; سوی شهر ایران is a directional prepositional phrase 'toward the city of Iran'; هواست is composed of هوا 'desire/wish' as subject and ست (short form of است) 'is' — together 'the desire is toward the city of Iran' or 'if your aim/desire is toward the Iranian city.'
segments.s286-g144: دل هر کسی = 'the heart of each person'; بر تنش uses بر here in the sense 'over/on' to indicate authority or rule 'over his body' (تن + ـش his); پادشاست is a nominal phrase 'is king' (پادشاه used as predicate). Literally 'the heart of each person is king over his body' — a proverbial assertion that each man's inclination rules his bodily actions.
segments.s286-g41: This is a noun phrase composed of a head and modifiers. دلیران ایران = “the brave (men) of Iran” (genitive relationship; classical Persian often omits an explicit ezāfe), گرازان is an appositive/epithet meaning “boar-like” applied to those brave men, and به هم attaches as a particle meaning “at/with one another / together,” preparing the mutual action expressed by the following verb.
segments.s286-g42: رسیدند is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural verb “they came/reached” (here best rendered as “fell upon/attacked”). یکسر is an adverb modifying the verb meaning “entirely / at once.” بر is the preposition “against/on,” governing the following name گستهم (here a proper name, Gostaham); note the poetically inverted order: verb + object. گستهم is a personal name, not the verbal sense listed in the lemma entries.
segments.s286-g43: وز here is an archaic/prepositional element (classical usage equivalent to از, “from”), introducing the source/location; آن سوی = “that side / beyond that point.” زیبد is likely a place-name (Zibad) or a localizing word, so it stands with آن سوی to mark origin. یکی تیره‌گرد = “a certain tîreh-gard” (a compound epithet meaning ‘dark-roving’ or ‘one that brings gloom’), where یکی is the indefinite pronoun/number.
segments.s286-g44: Two coordinated clauses with V+Aux order typical of epic diction: پدید آمد “appeared / came into view” (پدید = ‘appear’ + آمد = past ‘came’). The conjunction و links to دشت شد لاژورد, where شد is the past of شدن “to become” and the subject دشت (the plain) follows the verb (inverted order); لاژورد is a color/metaphor (“lapis/azure”), so دشت شد لاژورد = “the plain became azure.”
segments.s286-g45: میان + noun phrase is a locative/partitive prepositional frame: میان سپه کاویانی = “among/within the Kayanian army” (کاویانی = adjectival/ethnic qualifier). درفش follows as the main noun ‘banner/standard’ (so the phrase situates the banner within the Kayanian host). The order places the locative first for emphasis, typical in epic register.
segments.s286-g46: This is a locative/descriptive cluster modifying درفش. به پیش = “in front / before” (here marking position), اندرون = “inside/within,” and تیغهای بنفش is the noun phrase “purple swords” (تیغهای = plural with the plural morpheme, بنفش postnominal adjective). Together they describe the banner’s accoutrements/position: “before it, within, purple swords.”
segments.s286-g47: درفش شهنشاه is a compound/possessive expression meaning “the banner of the shahanshah (king of kings)” where the possessive relation is expressed by juxtaposition (classical Persian often omits an explicit ezāfe in poetry). با بوق و کوس is an instrumental/accompaniment phrase “with trumpet and drum,” specifying the sounds that accompany the banner.
segments.s286-g48: Again two coordinated verbal predicates: پدید آمد “appeared” (padeed + amad) and و شد “and became.” زمین (the earth/ground) follows the verb by poetic inversion and is predicated by آبنوس, a metaphorical adjective meaning ‘ebony/very dark,’ so the clause reads “and the earth became ebony” (a vivid image for darkness or crowding).
segments.s286-g129: وگر is the conditional particle 'if' (poetic). جنگ را is the object 'the war/host' plus the definite object marker را (object comes before the verb phrase). گرد کرده is a compound verbal phrase: گرد کردن + کرده (past participle) here meaning 'having gathered/rounded up' or 'having taken in'; عنان ('reins') is the direct object of گرد کردن and completes the verbal phrase. The whole phrase functions as an introductory conditional clause ('if having taken the reins of war').
segments.s286-g130: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one / each'; به + خوناب is a prepositional phrase 'into/to the blood' (به here = 'towards/into'); داده is the past participle 'given/has given' (here used with an implied agent or with agent placed after the verb in poetic inversion); سنان ('spear') is the agent placed after the verb for emphasis — a common poetic inversion (object/adjunct before verb, agent after).
segments.s286-g131: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدون is an archaic temporal/verb-form here ('when/now that it comes' or 'now'), used to introduce the time of the condition. کتان appears to be a proper name in context (so it functions as the possessor/subject whose دل 'heart' is affected) and therefore does not take the literal sense 'linen'. دل گراید is the core: دل (heart) + گراید (present-subjunctive/optative 'turns/will incline'), the verb expressing potential inclination introduced by the conditional.
segments.s286-g132: بدین here is the fused form 'بـ + دین' = 'with/at this' (poetic demonstrative); رزمگه is the locative noun 'battlefield'. کرد باید درنگ is an expression with کرد (do/make) + باید (must) + درنگ (a noun 'pause/delay'): together meaning 'one must make/there must be a pause' — note the older order where the light verb کرد combines with the noun درنگ and باید governs the whole predicate.
segments.s286-g133: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause 'that/for'. پیران is the subject 'the elders'. ز مهتر is a prepositional phrase 'from the chief' (ز = 'from'). سپه is the object 'an army/host', and خواسته است is the perfect construction (خواسته past participle + است auxiliary) meaning 'have requested/asked'. Word order is subject — prepositional source — object — perfect verb.
segments.s286-g134: سپهبد (spahbed) is the subject 'the (army) commander'. یکی before لشکر is an indefinite 'one/a certain' modifying 'laskhar' (army). آراسته است is another perfect/resultative (آراسته past participle + است) meaning 'has arrayed/assembled' — hence: 'the general has marshalled an army.' Word order places the indefinite before the noun for emphasis.
segments.s286-g135: زمان تا زمان is a reduplicative idiom 'from time to time' (literally 'time to time'). لشکر آید پدید is a verbal clause 'an army comes forth/appears' with آید (present/subjunctive 'comes') and the verbal/adverbial particle پدید ('forth/into view') following it; subject لشکر precedes the verb in the customary SOV/poetic order.
segments.s286-g136: همی is an emphatic adverb 'always/continually'. کینه ز ایشان is the object 'hatred/grudge from them' (object noun preceded by the source-marking preposition ز); بباید is the modal 'one must / it is necessary' (poetic form), and کشید is the main verb 'to draw/take' (here in an older poetic sense 'to wreak/bring forth' or 'to draw out/inflict' on them). The clause thus has the object preposed and uses the archaic modal + infinitive/past-stem order ('بباید کشید').
segments.s286-g97: چنین relative clause begins with که (here meaning 'who'/'he who') plus the object marker را after it — a classical/emphatic ordering common in epic Persian. دل دهد is a 3rd-person present/past-reporting form of دادن meaning 'gives (his) heart' (i.e. 'devotes himself'); نیز 'also' introduces a second coordinated action expressed by the following verbal noun بستن کمر ('to bind/gird the waist'). The verb دهد is the main finite verb governing the clause while the infinitival verbal noun names the additional action.
segments.s286-g98: ز آهن کله is a poetic/material phrase 'a head/helmet of iron' where ز + material marks composition ('of/from iron') and کله is the noun 'head' (here = helmet). برنهادن is an older compound verb meaning 'to set/raise upon', followed by the preposition به marking the target 'سر' ('on the head'). Grammatically this infinitival phrase (برنهادن به سر) parallels بستن کمر as another action attributed to the same subject.
segments.s286-g99: چنین 'thus' precedes the reporting verb گفت ('said'); in classical Persian the verb may appear before its subject, so 'چنین گفت لهاک و فرشیدورد' = 'Thus said Lahak and Farshidvard.' Here گفت is the past reporting verb and the two following words form the compound subject (proper names).
segments.s286-g100: The clause is a reported content clause introduced by که ('that'). از here means 'from' or 'by' indicating source: 'from the will of God' (خواست یزدان = 'the will/desire of God'). کرانه 'limit/edge' qualifies the manner (i.e. 'to the limit'), and the final که کرد ('that he did') contains کرد, the past tense of کردن 'to do,' completing the sense 'that which God willed, he did to the limit.'
segments.s286-g101: چنین 'thus' modifies the verb راند (past 'drove', 'cast') which comes before its subject روزگار ('fate'/'fortune'), a typical epic inversion. بر سر ورا is a compound prepositional phrase meaning 'upon his head' (بر = on; سر = head; ورا = to him/thither), indicating the thing fate brought upon him.
segments.s286-g102: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that ...'. بر کینه means 'because of/on account of hatred' (بر = on/against; کینه = hatred). کشته شود is a passive construction: کشته (the slain / killed) + شود (auxiliary 'be') = 'be killed'. The adjectives زار و خوار follow to describe the resulting state ('wretched and despised').
segments.s286-g103: به شمشیر is an instrumental phrase 'with/by the sword' (به here used as the instrumental 'by'). کرده جدا is a past participial construction 'having made/separated apart' (کرده = done/having done; جدا = apart/separated). سر ز تن ('head from body') gives the object that was separated: 'the head from the body.'
segments.s286-g104: نیابد is an archaic negative/negative-potential form of یافتن 'to find' meaning 'does not/will not find'; همی is an adverb of continuity/emphasis ('ever/indeed'). کشته here is a substantive 'the slain', and گور و کفن are the objects 'grave and shroud' — so the phrase states that the slain will not gain/find grave nor shroud.
segments.s286-g105: به هرجای is a locative prepositional phrase meaning 'to/into every place' (wherever). کشته is a noun 'the slain' and کشان is a present‑participle/agentive ending meaning 'dragging' (forming the compound 'کشته‌کشان' = 'those who drag the slain'). دشمنش is 'his enemy' with the enclitic -ش marking 'his'. The whole chunk reads literally 'to wherever the slain‑dragging his enemy (took them)'.
segments.s286-g106: پر از (full of) is a fixed adjectival phrase: پر 'full' + از 'of'. A coordinated list follows: سر و درع و خسته تنش 'head and armor and his weary body' — note تنش is تن + enclitic -ش 'his'. The adjective phrase (پر از خون) applies to these coordinated nouns.
segments.s286-g107: کنون 'now' sets the time. بودنی is a deverbal noun 'being, existence' and is followed by بود, the past copula 'was' (so 'there was being' / 'life existed now'). و 'and' links the clause to پیران گذشت, where پیران 'the elders' is the subject and گذشت is the past verb 'passed' (i.e. they passed away or went by).
segments.s286-g108: همه کار و کردار او is 'all his work and conduct' (همه 'all' modifying the coordinated nouns کار و کردار, او 'his'). باد گشت is an optative/poetic construction: باد (optative particle 'may/let') + گشت (past of گشتن 'become/turn'), literally 'may ... become wind' — an idiom meaning 'may it be blown away' or 'be gone'.
segments.s286-g109: ستون سپه is a nominal phrase 'pillar/support of the army' (ستون 'pillar/support' + سپه 'army'). بود is the past copula 'was'. تا followed by زنده بود ('was alive') gives the temporal clause 'until/while he was alive' — i.e. 'he was the army's pillar as long as he lived.'
segments.s286-g110: به مهر سپه is an adverbial phrase 'with/by the love of the army' (به + مهر 'love/affection' + سپه 'army'), modifying the predicate. جانش is جان 'life' with the enclitic -ش 'his' ('his life'). آگنده is a predicative adjective 'filled' and بود is the copula 'was' — 'his life was filled with love for the host.'
segments.s286-g111: سپه را uses the object/topic marker را to topicalize 'the army' (literary word order); ز دشمن 'from/against the enemy' gives the adversary/source. نگهدار is a noun 'guardian/preserver' and بود is the copula 'was'. The structure reads like 'a guardian of the army against the enemy there was.'
segments.s286-g112: پسر با برادر 'son with brother' lists relatives; the following برش is an enclitic/clitic sequence (بر + ش) whose exact historical sense here functions like a dative/benefactive 'to/for him' and is not one of the literal senses listed in the word‑info table, so its lexical sense is irregular/elliptical. خوار is an adjective 'despised/worthless' and بود is the copula 'was' — overall: 'his son and brother were of no account to him.'
segments.s286-g145: ز ما دو برادر مدارید چشم is an idiomatic phrase: ز + ما = toward/from us; دو برادر is the noun phrase 'two brothers'; مدارید is a second-person plural present/imperative/subjunctive form meaning 'regard/be lenient' or 'show mercy' and is addressed to someone (imperative/request); چشم ('eye') is used idiomatically ('with your eye' = 'with your glance/favour') and functions as the object of مدارید. Word order is topical (ز ما ... چشم) common in classical Persian.
segments.s286-g146: که introduces a result/consequence ('for/that'), هرگز is an emphatic negator 'never'. نشوییم is 1st-person-plural negative subjunctive/optative ('we may not be washed/cleansed' or 'we shall never be cleansed') used here figuratively. دل را uses را as the definite object marker ('the heart' as the thing affected). ز خشم is a prepositional phrase 'from/because of anger' and gives the source or content of the cleansing.
segments.s286-g147: کز is the archaic/poetic form of از/که introducing a clause ('from/that'); این تخمه ویسگان is a genitive construction: این تخمه = 'this seed/stock/offspring' and ویسگان names the clan/household ('of Vis'); کس نبود is an existential negation 'there was no one' (کس as 'no one' and نبود = past 'was not').
segments.s286-g148: که is a relative 'who/that'. بند کمر = 'the band/girdle of the waist' (بند as 'bond/girdle', کمر 'waist'). بر میانش = 'over/around his middle' (بر = on/upon, میانش = his middle). نسود is an archaic/past-form verb meaning 'perished/wore away/was consumed' — so the relative clause says the belt 'wore out' on that person's middle. In combination with the previous negative, the sense is that there was nobody for whom the belt did not perish (i.e., it perished for everyone).
segments.s286-g149: بر اندرز = 'in/at the advice' or 'heeding the counsel' where بر is a preposition 'on/upon' used with an abstract noun. سالار پیران is a title-like genitive: سالار 'commander/chief' of پیران 'the elders' (i.e. 'the chief of the elders'). شویم is 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive/epic past-form of شدن ('become' / 'we did/become'), here idiomatically 'we obeyed/heeded' or 'we acted on' the counsel.
segments.s286-g150: ز راه بیابان forms a prepositional phrase 'by/along the desert road' (ز = 'from/by/through', راه = 'road', بیابان = 'desert'). به توران شویم — به 'toward/to' + proper name توران, and شویم repeats the 1st-person-plural verb 'we went/proceeded' (classical use of شدن for movement/state). The two halves are coordinate: they heed counsel and then proceed by the desert road to Turan.
segments.s286-g151: ار is the archaic conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an uncertain/modal particle meaning roughly 'perhaps' or 'in that case'. The verb phrase is بر ما بگیرند راه where بگیرند is 3rd-person-plural subjunctive ('they should seize/take'); بر ما indicates direction/target ('against us') and راه is the object 'the road' — together 'they take/seize the road against us' i.e. 'they block our way'.
segments.s286-g152: بکوشیم is 1st-person-plural jussive/subjunctive 'let us strive' / 'we will strive'. تا here functions temporally/conditionaly as 'while/until' (we will strive so long as ...). هستمان is a fused form 'هست + مان' meaning 'our being/existence' or 'while we have (being/strength)'; دستگاه is 'establishment/organization/power' so هستمان دستگاه = 'while our power/establishment exists'. The clause expresses determination to strive as long as their force remains.
segments.s286-g65: This is a fronted prepositional/topic phrase: از این شهر (from this city) places the place first, followed by the nominal subject ترکان و افراسیاب (the Turks and Afrasiab). Persian allows the locative phrase to come before the subject for emphasis.
segments.s286-g66: بد آمد is a light-clause meaning 'an evil thing came / befell'; سرانجامت (your outcome) is the thing that 'came' and is marked by the possessive suffix -ت. ای نیک‌یاب is a vocative: ای + epithet/name addressing the addressee. Word order is emphatic (adjective/numeral + آمد + affected noun).
segments.s286-g67: بباید + بردن (here بریدن) is a modal construction meaning 'it is necessary / one must ...'. بریدن is the infinitive 'to cut / to behead', and سر خویش (head his-own) is the direct object; the adjective پست follows the noun and modifies it ('despicable / lowly head'). The construction is impersonal/agentless (it is necessary that his head be cut).
segments.s286-g68: This is a verbal-noun + light-verb phrase: به خون غرقه کردن = 'to make (someone/something) drenched in blood' (به + noun expresses the medium/into which), where غرقه is a participial/adjectival form and کردن is the light verb. بر یال و دست marks the targets with بر + coordinate nouns (yāl, دست) after the verb phrase.
segments.s286-g69: چو here introduces a temporal clause 'when'. اندرز پیران is the noun phrase 'the elders' counsel' and نهادند is the past transitive 'they placed/put (before)'; پیش (before/in front of) indicates the advice was set in front of (them). Word order is typical old/poetic Persian: temporal particle + object + verb + locative.
segments.s286-g70: نرفتند is past negative 'they did not go / they did not yield/turn' (here 'did not abandon / did not change'). بر introduces the domain/target (their obstinate words), گفتار خویش = 'their own speech/words' (خوی + possessive ش = 'their own'). خیره is an evaluative adjective modifying گفتار (poetically 'stubborn/blind/obstinate'); together the phrase means 'they did not turn away from their obstinate words.'
segments.s286-g71: ز here is the poetic/genitive/agentive preposition 'from/by' introducing the agent Goudarz; چو introduces 'when'. خواست is past 'he wanted/asked/summoned'; پیران (elders) is the object of that seeking and نبرد 'battle' gives the purpose/occasion. The clause means 'when Goudarz sought/summoned the elders about the battle.'
segments.s286-g72: چنین گفت is the formula 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus'). با + گرد + فرشیدورد is addressed to a group: با normally 'with/to' and گرد here is the archaic/poetic noun 'retinue / band/host' (i.e. Farshidvard's troop). فرشیدورد is the personal name. Note: some lemmas in the supplied sense lists do not include the exact verbal/noun senses used here, so sense indices reflect that where appropriate.
segments.s286-g33: This is a simple nominal subject + verb + two prepositional phrases. 'سپاه' is the subject ('the army'). 'اندر آمد' is a poetic/periphrastic past action 'came in/arrived' (here meaning 'came down/advanced'). 'ز بالا' is an ablative phrase 'from above/from the heights' and 'به پست' is a goal/locative 'to the low (ground)'; the preposition 'اندر' before the verb is an archaic/poetic placement.
segments.s286-g34: 'خروشان' is a present‑participle/adjectival adverb ('roaring,tumultuous') describing the army's action. 'و' is the coordinating conjunction 'and.' 'هر یک' is a distributive phrase 'each (one)'. 'درفشی به دست' is an idiomatic phrase 'a banner in (his) hand' — 'درفشی' = banner/standard and 'به دست' = 'in hand' (instrumental). Word order places the descriptive 'خروشان' before the conjunction that begins the next clause.
segments.s286-g35: A noun phrase with postpositive modifiers. 'درفش' (the banner/standard) is followed by the title 'سپهدار توران' ('the commander/chief of Turan'), and 'نگون' is a poetic adjective placed after the noun meaning 'overthrown/ill‑fated' describing that standard.
segments.s286-g36: 'همی' is the frequentative/continuous particle (common in epic Persian) giving an ongoing sense; 'بینم' is 1st‑person present 'I see/I behold.' 'از پیش' functions adverbially as 'before me/in front (of me)/in my sight.' 'غرقه به خون' is a predicative adjective phrase 'drenched in blood' with the past‑participle 'غرقه' plus 'به' + 'خون' to mark the medium ('in/with blood').
segments.s286-g37: 'همان ده دلاور' is a fixed noun phrase 'those ten warriors/ten brave ones' (همان = those/the same + ده = ten + دلاور = brave men). 'کز' is the relative pronoun 'who/which/that,' 'ایدر' = 'here,' and 'برفت' is a past verb 'departed/went away' (archaic/epic past form).
segments.s286-g38: 'ابا' here denotes reluctance/refusal (they were unwilling or showed reluctance). 'گرد پیران' literally 'around the elders' (گرد used adverbially = 'about/around' or 'the circle of'), 'به آورد' is a compound verb 'brought about/caused,' and 'تفت' is a noun/image meaning 'heat/scorching' used metaphorically for fury or violent action. Together: they, showing reluctance before the elders, caused/brought forth a burning/fury.
segments.s286-g39: 'همی' again is the continuous/frequentative particle and 'بینم' is 'I see.' 'از دورشان' is an adverbial phrase 'from afar/from their distance' (i.e., 'I see them at a distance'), and 'سرنگون' is a predicate adjective meaning 'overthrown/cast down' describing the people seen.
segments.s286-g40: 'فگنده' is a past-participial adjective 'thrown/laid' (here 'hurled/struck down'), 'بر اسبان' = 'upon the horses' (بر = on/upon + اسبان = horses). 'و' = 'and.' 'تن پر ز خون' is an adjectival phrase 'bodies full of blood' where 'تن' = body, 'پر' = full (of), and 'ز خون' = 'with/of blood' (poetic use of 'ز').

sentences.s286-s40: For when they kill us at the seat of hatred, they will carry our heads toward Iran.
